explanation: The requirement says that whenever prop_0 becomes true, it must be followed by prop_1, then immediately prop_2, then quickly (with no delay or with minimal delay) prop_3. 'Immediately' for prop_2 after prop_1 suggests that in the step directly after prop_1, prop_2 holds, and similarly for prop_3 after prop_2 ('quickly' usually mapped to next, unless specified in more detail).

This requirement indicates that whenever prop_0 occurs, the system should respond with prop_1, and immediately with prop_2, then quickly (which in LTL is often interpreted as 'next') with prop_3. In LTL, this means: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 holds, prop_2 holds in the same instant, and prop_3 in the next instant. This is captured by the formula: Globally, if prop_0, then prop_1 and prop_2 and next prop_3.

The requirement says the system must perform prop_0 as a result of (i.e., induced by) both prop_1 AND prop_2, and also prop_3. Since the statement is a bit ambiguous, but appears to require: whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 and prop_3 occur, then prop_0 shall happen (eventually, or simultaneously). The usual LTL translation is: whenever the specified conditions (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3) become true, eventually prop_0 must be true.
output_LTL: G(((P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> inspiratoryPhaseEnd) & (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> expiratoryPhaseStart) & (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen))) -> F (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> highPriorityAlarm))

explanation: The requirement specifies that 'prop_0' should occur by the time 'prop_1' happens. Then, immediately after 'prop_1', 'prop_2' must occur. After that, 'prop_3' must quickly follow (which, in LTL, is best represented as 'next').

The requirement is that prop_0 should be accomplished or satisfied by prop_1 and prop_2 together with prop_3. This suggests that whenever prop_1 and prop_2 and prop_3 all hold, then prop_0 must happen (i.e. prop_0 is guaranteed whenever the other three are all true) This gives a constraint: G((prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3) -> prop_0).

The requirement specifies a sequential order: prop_0 must be caused by (i.e., is preceded by) prop_1, must be immediately followed by prop_2, and then quickly by prop_3. In LTL, 'immediately' suggests the 'next' operator, and 'quickly' can also be interpreted as either immediately or at least the next step (we will assume it means 'next'). Therefore, once prop_0 occurs, in the next state prop_2 must occur, and in the following state prop_3 must occur. Furthermore, prop_1 must occur before prop_0 (they may occur simultaneously or prop_1 before prop_0, depending on stricter interpretation).

This requirement means that whenever prop_0 holds (the alarm for Max P insp), the system shall respond by eventually (at some point) performing prop_1 and then both prop_2 and prop_3. It is not strictly specified if prop_2 and prop_3 must occur together or if their ordering matters, but a literal reading suggests that after prop_1, both prop_2 and prop_3 should happen (possibly at the same time). This can be expressed as: globally, if prop_0 happens, then eventually prop_1 will happen, and eventually (after prop_1) both prop_2 and prop_3 will happen.

The statement says that the system shall prop_0 by prop_1 and prop_2, prop_3. This suggests that the occurrence of prop_0 should be caused/preceded/guaranteed by prop_1 and prop_2, prop_3. Most likely, the intention is that if prop_0 occurs, it is after prop_1 and (prop_2 or prop_3) have occurred; this is naturally expressed as: prop_0 implies that previously, prop_1 and (prop_2 or prop_3) have both occurred at some point. But in standard LTL, enforcing 'caused by' expresses as 'if prop_0 then F(p1 & (p2 | p3))' (sometime after prop_0, prop_1 and (prop_2 or prop_3) occur). Alternatively, requiring that whenever prop_1 and (prop_2 or prop_3) both occur, prop_0 shall eventually follow. We'll encode the more commonly used: If prop_1 and (prop_2 or prop_3), then eventually prop_0.

When prop_3 occurs, the system must react with prop_0 and immediately with prop_1, followed quickly by prop_2. 'Immediately' is interpreted as 'next' (i.e., X), and 'quickly' as within the next two steps (i.e., either in the same state or the next state). Therefore, whenever prop_3 is true, prop_0 must hold, prop_1 must hold in the next state, and prop_2 must hold in the next or next-next state.
output_LTL: G ((P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp) -> ((P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp | V_E_lessThan_ExpiratoryTriggerSensitivity_multiplyBy_PeakV_E | l1_lessThan_ExpiratoryTriggerSensitivity) & X (inspiratoryPhaseEnd | expiratoryPhaseStart) & ((outValveOpen & inValveClose) | X (outValveOpen & inValveClose))))

explanation: When prop_1 becomes true, immediately after it, prop_2 must become true and soon after that (quickly), prop_3 must become true. Also, at some point, prop_0 must hold (likely at the start or at the occurrence of the other propositions, as per the requirement). The phrase 'immediately' is mapped to the 'next' operator, while 'quickly' can be modeled as an 'eventually within 1 step', which is the F[0:1] operator. This captures that prop_3 should happen either at the same time as prop_2 or in the next step after prop_2. The basic structure is that if prop_1 occurs, then next prop_2 occurs, and within one more step, prop_3 occurs. prop_0 is also required, perhaps globally or at the same time as prop_2/prop_3. For generality, it can be required globally.
output_LTL: G( (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp) -> X((P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp | V_E_lessThan_ExpiratoryTriggerSensitivity_multiplyBy_PeakV_E | l1_lessThan_ExpiratoryTriggerSensitivity | inspiratoryPhaseEnd) & F[0:1] (outValveOpen & inValveClose)) ) & G (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp)
